question: [File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getTokenAccounts()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `rows.map((row) => new TokenAccount(row))` creates a new `TokenAccount` object for every row returned, and by querying accounts with the maximum number of token associations at maximum limit repeatedly, cause excessive garbage collection pressure in Node.js that degrades event loop throughput and causes a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getCachedTokens()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `rows.forEach((row) => { tokenCache.set(tokenId, cachedToken); cachedTokens.set(tokenId, cachedToken); })` in `getCachedTokens()` performs two Map/LRU set operations per row, and by triggering responses with 100 rows of uncached tokens per request at high concurrency, cause the quickLru internal linked-list reordering to become a CPU bottleneck and cause a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getTokenRelationshipsQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an `order` parameter value that, when passed to `OrderSpec.from(TokenAccount.getFullName(TokenAccount.TOKEN_ID), order)`, produces an ORDER BY clause that prevents the DB from using an index on `token_id`, forcing a full sort operation on the `token_account` table and causing excessive DB CPU usage, leading to a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getInClauseSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply 100 identical `token.id=eq:X` parameters (same value repeated), causing `tokenIdInParams` to contain 100 duplicate entries that are all interpolated into the SQL IN clause as `in (X,X,X,...,X)`, causing the DB to evaluate a degenerate IN clause with 100 identical values and wasting DB CPU cycles, contributing to a non-network-based DoS under high concurrency?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getCachedTokens()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `tokenCache` is initialized once at module load with `new quickLru({ maxSize: config.cache.token.maxSize })` and never reset (except in test environments via `clearTokenCache()`), to permanently fill the cache with 100,000 low-value token entries that crowd out high-value tokens, causing sustained cache miss rates for legitimate traffic and a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getTokenAccounts()] Can an unprivileged external user cause `super.getRows(sqlQuery, params)` in `getTokenAccounts()` to return a result set where all `row.token_id` values are identical (due to a DB anomaly or crafted data), causing `rows.reduce((result, row) => result.add(row.token_id), new Set())` to produce a Set of size 1, then `getCachedTokens()` to issue a `token_id = any($1)` query with a single-element array, and `rows.map()` to still iterate all 100 rows — creating an asymmetric workload that wastes CPU?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getTokenRelationshipsQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the hardcoded `super.getLimitQuery(2)` call which always uses parameter position `$2` for the LIMIT clause, and by supplying a crafted `limit` value that is the maximum integer allowed, cause the DB to attempt to allocate result set buffers for an extremely large LIMIT, causing DB memory exhaustion and a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getCachedTokens()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential (non-parallel) nature of `getCachedTokens()` — which first iterates `tokenIds` to check the cache, then awaits a DB query, then iterates DB rows — to hold a DB connection for the entire duration of both iterations plus the query, and by sending many concurrent requests, exhaust the DB connection pool and cause a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getTokenAccounts()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger `getTokenAccounts()` for an account with no token associations (returning `rows.length === 0`) at extremely high request rates, causing the early-return path `return []` to be hit but still consuming a DB connection for `tokenRelationshipsQuery` on every request, and with the 10-connection pool exhausted by concurrent requests, causing a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getInClauseSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply `token.id=eq:` parameters with very large numeric values (e.g., `9999999999999999999`) that when coerced to string via `tokenIdInParams.toString()` produce a very long SQL IN clause string, causing the DB to spend excessive time parsing the query string and a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getCachedTokens()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `new Map()` is allocated on every call to `getCachedTokens()` and `new Array()` for `uncachedTokenIds`, and by triggering thousands of concurrent `getCachedTokens()` calls, cause excessive heap allocation and GC pressure in Node.js that degrades event loop responsiveness and causes a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getTokenRelationshipsQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply conditions with `condition.key` values that, when passed to `TokenAccount.getFullName(condition.key)`, produce column references that are not indexed in the `token_account` table, causing the DB to perform full table scans for every request and a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getTokenAccounts()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `getTokenAccounts()` calls `this.getTokenRelationshipsQuery(query)` synchronously (CPU-bound string building) before the async DB call, and by sending many concurrent requests, cause the synchronous string concatenation in `getTokenRelationshipsQuery()` to block the Node.js event loop and cause a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getCachedTokens()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `tokenCache.get(tokenId)` in `getCachedTokens()` is called inside a `forEach` loop with no async/await, meaning all cache lookups are synchronous and block the event loop for large `tokenIds` Sets, and by crafting requests that produce large `tokenIds` Sets, cause event loop starvation and a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: putTokenCache()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger `putTokenCache()` with a token object where `token.decimals` is an extremely large number or a specially crafted object, causing `new CachedToken(token)` to store a large value in the cache that consumes disproportionate memory per entry, and by filling the cache with 100,000 such entries, exhaust Node.js heap memory and cause a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getTokenRelationshipsQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `conditions` array with 100 entries (via `maxRepeatedQueryParameters: 100`) each pushing a value into `params`, causing `params` to grow to 102 entries and the SQL query to have 102 bind parameters, and by sending many such requests concurrently, cause the DB to spend excessive time parsing and binding parameters, leading to a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getCachedTokens()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `tokenCache` uses quickLru which maintains an internal doubly-linked list for LRU ordering, and by triggering many `tokenCache.get()` calls that hit cached entries (causing LRU reordering), cause the linked-list pointer updates to become a CPU bottleneck under high concurrency, degrading Node.js throughput and causing a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getTokenAccounts()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `rows.map((row) => new TokenAccount(row))` creates a new `TokenAccount` object for every row returned, and by querying accounts with the maximum number of token associations at maximum limit repeatedly, cause excessive garbage collection pressure in Node.js that degrades event loop throughput and causes a non-network-based DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getCachedTokens()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `tokenCache` uses quickLru which maintains an internal doubly-linked list for LRU ordering, and by triggering many `tokenCache.get()` calls that hit cached entries (causing LRU reordering), cause the linked-list pointer updates to become a CPU bottleneck under high concurrency, degrading Node.js throughput and causing a non-network-based DoS?
